context:
Section 6 (Discussion), page 16

To begin with, it remains unclear how the DDPM sampler adapts to unknown low dimensionality when other metrics (e.g., the total-variation distance or the Wasserstein distance) are used to measure distributional discrepancy.
title: Convergence and low-dimensional adaptivity of DDPM under non-KL metrics (e.g., total variation or Wasserstein)
background_latex: Let \(d\in\mathbb{N}\). Let \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) be a probability distribution on \(\mathbb{R}^d\) with support \(\mathcal{X}_{\mathrm{data}}\subseteq \mathbb{R}^d\) having intrinsic dimension \(k\) in the sense that its metric entropy is controlled by \(\log N_{\mathrm{cover}}(\mathcal{X}_{\mathrm{data}},\|\cdot\|_2,\varepsilon_0)\lesssim k\log(1/\varepsilon_0)\) for a small scale \(\varepsilon_0\). Consider the Ornstein–Uhlenbeck forward process \(X_0\sim p_{\mathrm{data}}\) and \(X_t\stackrel{d}{=}\sqrt{1-\sigma_t^2}\,X_0+\sigma_t W\) with \(W\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I_d)\) independent and \(\sigma_t=\sqrt{1-e^{-2t}}\), so the marginal law of \(X_t\) is denoted \(q_t\). Let \(s_t(x)=\nabla \log q_t(x)\) be the (Stein) score. Fix a time horizon \(T>0\) and a discretization \(0=t_0<t_1<\cdots<t_N=T\). The DDPM sampler produces iterates \((Y_{t_n})_{n=0}^N\) with \(Y_{t_0}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I_d)\) and the standard DDPM update (as in Ho et al. (2020)) using (possibly perfect) score access \(s_{T-t_n}(\cdot)\); denote the output law by \(p_{\mathrm{out}}:=\mathcal{L}(Y_{t_N})\). For an early stopping time \(\delta\in(0,1)\), the benchmark target is \(q_\delta\), the law of \(X_\delta\) (i.e., \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) convolved with small Gaussian noise). Let \(d_{\mathrm{TV}}(\cdot,\cdot)\) denote total variation distance and let \(W_2(\cdot,\cdot)\) denote the 2-Wasserstein distance.
problem_definition_latex: Determine whether, and under what verifiable assumptions on \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) and the score \(s_t\), the DDPM output \(p_{\mathrm{out}}\) achieves convergence to \(q_\delta\) with iteration complexity scaling nearly linearly in the intrinsic dimension \(k\) (up to logarithmic factors) when the discrepancy is measured by \(d_{\mathrm{TV}}(q_\delta,p_{\mathrm{out}})\) and/or \(W_2(q_\delta,p_{\mathrm{out}})\), rather than by \(\mathrm{KL}(q_\delta\|p_{\mathrm{out}})\).
latex:
Let \(d\in\mathbb{N}\). Let \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) be a probability distribution on \(\mathbb{R}^d\) with support \(\mathcal{X}_{\mathrm{data}}\subseteq \mathbb{R}^d\) having intrinsic dimension \(k\) in the sense that its metric entropy is controlled by \(\log N_{\mathrm{cover}}(\mathcal{X}_{\mathrm{data}},\|\cdot\|_2,\varepsilon_0)\lesssim k\log(1/\varepsilon_0)\) for a small scale \(\varepsilon_0\). Consider the Ornstein–Uhlenbeck forward process \(X_0\sim p_{\mathrm{data}}\) and \(X_t\stackrel{d}{=}\sqrt{1-\sigma_t^2}\,X_0+\sigma_t W\) with \(W\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I_d)\) independent and \(\sigma_t=\sqrt{1-e^{-2t}}\), so the marginal law of \(X_t\) is denoted \(q_t\). Let \(s_t(x)=\nabla \log q_t(x)\) be the (Stein) score. Fix a time horizon \(T>0\) and a discretization \(0=t_0<t_1<\cdots<t_N=T\). The DDPM sampler produces iterates \((Y_{t_n})_{n=0}^N\) with \(Y_{t_0}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I_d)\) and the standard DDPM update (as in Ho et al. (2020)) using (possibly perfect) score access \(s_{T-t_n}(\cdot)\); denote the output law by \(p_{\mathrm{out}}:=\mathcal{L}(Y_{t_N})\). For an early stopping time \(\delta\in(0,1)\), the benchmark target is \(q_\delta\), the law of \(X_\delta\) (i.e., \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) convolved with small Gaussian noise). Let \(d_{\mathrm{TV}}(\cdot,\cdot)\) denote total variation distance and let \(W_2(\cdot,\cdot)\) denote the 2-Wasserstein distance.

Determine whether, and under what verifiable assumptions on \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) and the score \(s_t\), the DDPM output \(p_{\mathrm{out}}\) achieves convergence to \(q_\delta\) with iteration complexity scaling nearly linearly in the intrinsic dimension \(k\) (up to logarithmic factors) when the discrepancy is measured by \(d_{\mathrm{TV}}(q_\delta,p_{\mathrm{out}})\) and/or \(W_2(q_\delta,p_{\mathrm{out}})\), rather than by \(\mathrm{KL}(q_\delta\|p_{\mathrm{out}})\).
open_problem_quote: To begin with, it remains unclear how the DDPM sampler adapts to unknown low dimensionality when other metrics (e.g., the total-variation distance or the Wasserstein distance) are used to measure distributional discrepancy.

verification.notice: This entry was generated from the paper text only. It has not been independently verified as still open. If you believe the question is solved or incorrectly stated, please contact the maintainers.
